perturbation.original_text: (xi) Waiver of Jury Trial. EACH PARTY IRREVOCABLY WAIVES ANY AND ALL RIGHT TO TRIAL BY JURY IN ANY LEGAL PROCEEDING IN CONNECTION WITH THIS AGREEMENT, ANY CREDIT SUPPORT DOCUMENT TO WHICH IT IS A PARTY, OR ANY TRANSACTION. EACH PARTY ALSO ACKNOWLEDGES THAT THIS WAIVER IS A MATERIAL INDUCEMENT TO THE OTHER PARTY'S ENTERING INTO THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: (xi) Waiver of Jury Trial. EACH PARTY WAIVES ANY RIGHT TO TRIAL BY JURY IN ANY LEGAL PROCEEDING IN CONNECTION WITH THIS AGREEMENT, ANY CREDIT SUPPORT DOCUMENT TO WHICH IT IS A PARTY, OR ANY TRANSACTION.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the word 'irrevocably' weakens the jury trial waiver. This introduces ambiguity about the enforceability of the waiver and could be challenged, especially in jurisdictions that require waivers to be knowing, voluntary, and intelligent. New York law has specific rules about the prominence and clarity of jury waivers. The initial clause stated that the parties 'irrevocably' waive their rights to trial, thus further showing their commitment and agreement to the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Constitution Article I, § 2, guarantees the right to a jury trial. While this right can be waived, the waiver must be knowing, intelligent, and voluntary. The original clause included the word 'irrevocably', and emphasized that the waiver was a 'material inducement', strengthening the argument that the waiver was intentional and understood. By removing 'irrevocably' it makes it easier to argue that the waiver was not fully understood or voluntary.

Article I, New York Constitution From Ballotpedia Jump to: navigation , search New York Constitution Preamble Articles I • II • III • IV • V • VI • VII • VIII • IX • X • XI • XII • XIII • XIV • XV • XVI • XVII • XVIII • XIX • XX Contents 1 Section 1 1.1 Amendments 2 Section 2 2.1 Amendments 3 Section 3 3.1 Amendments 4 Section 4 4.1 Amendments 5 Section 5 6 Section 6 6.1 Amendments 7 Section 7 7.1 Amendments 8 Section 8 8.1 Amendments 9 Section 9 9.1 Amendments 10 Section 10 11 Section 11 11.1 Amendments 12 Section 12 12.1 Amendments 13 Section 13 14 Section 14 14.1 Amendments 15 Section 15 16 Section 16 16.1 Amendments 17 Section 17 17.1 Amendments 18 Section 18 18.1 Amendments 19 Section 19 19.1 Amendments 20 See also 21 External links 22 Additional reading 23 Footnotes Article I of the New York Constitution is entitled Bill of Rights and consists of 18 sections, three of which have been repealed. Section 1 Text of Section 1: Rights, Privileges and Franchise Secured; Power of Legislature to Dispense with Primary Elections in Certain Cases No member of this state shall be disfranchised, or deprived of any of the rights or privileges secured to any citizen thereof, unless by the law of the land, or the judgment of his or her peers, except that the legislature may provide that there shall be no primary election held to nominate candidates for public office or to elect persons to party positions for any political party or parties in any unit of representation of the state from which such candidates or persons are nominated or elected whenever there is no contest or contests for such nominations or election as may be prescribed by general law. [1] Amendments Amended by vote of the people on November 3, 1959. Amended by vote of the people on November 6, 2001. Section 2 Text of Section 2: Trial by Jury; How Waived Trial by jury in all cases in which it has heretofore been guaranteed by constitutional provision shall remain inviolate forever; but a jury trial may be waived by the parties in all civil cases in the manner to be prescribed by law. The legislature may provide, however, by law, that a verdict may be rendered by not less than five-sixths of the jury in any civil case. A jury trial may be waived by the defendant in all cri
perturbation.justification: The removal of 'irrevocably' from the jury trial waiver introduces a legal contradiction. New York Constitution Article I, § 2, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 2, states that 'a jury trial may be waived by the parties in all civil cases in the manner to be prescribed by law.' The original text, by using 'irrevocably,' demonstrated a clear intent to waive this right. The modified text weakens this waiver, potentially allowing a party to argue that the waiver was not fully understood or voluntary, contradicting the requirement for a knowing and voluntary waiver. The Law Explanation highlights how the original clause strengthened the argument for an intentional waiver.

perturbation.original_text: (xiii) Governing Law. The parties to this Agreement hereby agree that the law of the State of New York shall govern their rights and duties in whole without regard to the conflict of law provisions thereof (other than New York General Obligations Law Sections 5-1401 and 5-1402).
perturbation.changed_text: (xiii) Governing Law. The parties to this Agreement hereby agree that the law of the State of New York shall govern their rights and duties.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "without regard to the conflict of law provisions thereof (other than New York General Obligations Law Sections 5-1401 and 5-1402)", the contract now becomes potentially ambiguous regarding which specific laws apply, and introduces conflict of laws principles that might allow for the application of other jurisdictions' laws which may conflict with certain consumer protections or mandatory provisions of New York law that would otherwise apply. New York General Obligations Law Sections 5-1401 and 5-1402 are particularly important when the agreement contains a choice of law provision favoring New York.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York General Obligations Law Sections 5-1401 and 5-1402
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law Section 5-1401 allows parties to contractually agree that New York law governs their agreement, even if one party has no reasonable relation to the state, provided the agreement involves at least $250,000. Omitting the phrase 'without regard to the conflict of law provisions thereof' makes the application of New York law less certain, potentially circumventing this provision and introducing uncertainty about which laws apply, thereby weakening the protections that the parties wanted under New York law. Omitting the caveat about sections 5-1401 and 5-1402 renders the 'Governing Law' clause incomplete and potentially misleading, since those statutes directly pertain to the enforceability of the governing law provision itself.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions up TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions next SECTION 5-1402 Choice of forum This entry was published on 2018-08-31 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The omission of "without regard to the conflict of law provisions thereof (other than New York General Obligations Law Sections 5-1401 and 5-1402)" creates a legal contradiction. As per the Law Explanation, omitting this phrase makes the application of New York law less certain, potentially circumventing Section 5-1401, which allows parties to contractually agree that New York law governs their agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states in SECTION 5-1401 that "the parties to any contract...may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties." By removing the explicit exclusion of conflict of law provisions, the changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens the intended certainty of New York law's application. This falls under Omission Legal Contradiction, as defined. Therefore, the contradiction is direct and undermines the protection afforded by New York law, thus weakening the governing law provision.

perturbation.original_text: (xv) Set-Off. Notwithstanding any provision of this Agreement or any other existing or future agreement, each party irrevocably waives any and all rights it may have to set off, net, recoup or otherwise withhold or suspend or condition payment or performance of any obligation between it and the other party hereunder against any obligation between it and the other party under any other agreements.
perturbation.changed_text: (xv) Set-Off. Notwithstanding any provision of this Agreement or any other existing or future agreement, each party waives any and all rights it may have to set off, net, recoup or otherwise withhold or suspend or condition payment or performance of any obligation between it and the other party hereunder against any obligation between it and the other party under any other agreements.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'irrevocably' weakens the waiver of set-off rights. This makes the enforceability of the waiver questionable, especially if one party later argues that the waiver was not fully understood or intended to be permanent. New York law requires waivers of contractual rights to be clear and unambiguous, and the original clause strengthened this.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Debtor and Creditor Law § 151
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Debtor and Creditor Law § 151 provides for setoff rights in certain situations, particularly those involving insolvency or bankruptcy. The original clause, by stating 'irrevocably waives,' aimed to provide a clear and unambiguous preclusion of these setoff rights. By removing 'irrevocably,' the contract creates a potential ambiguity that could allow a party to later argue that they did not intend to permanently waive their setoff rights, especially in the context of financial distress or bankruptcy proceedings. The inclusion of 'irrevocably' would show the intent for the contract to hold, even if insolvency issues arise.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 12 Debtor & Creditor ARTICLE 6-A Right of Set Off Against Unmatured Debts up ARTICLE 6-A Right of Set Off Against Unmatured Debts This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2019-12-13 2020-04-10 2021-05-14 2021-08-06 2021-10-29 2022-07-29 Share Facebook Email SECTION 151 Right of set off against unmatured debts Debtor & Creditor (DCD) CHAPTER 12, ARTICLE 6-A § 151. Right of set off against unmatured debts. Every debtor shall have the right upon: (a) the filing of a petition under any of the provisions of the federal bankruptcy act or amendments thereto or the commencement of any proceeding under any foreign bankruptcy, insolvency, debtor relief or other similar statute or body of law, by or against a creditor; (b) the making of an assignment by a creditor for the benefit of its creditors; (c) the application for the appointment, or the appointment, of any receiver of, or of any of the property of a creditor; (d) the issuance of any execution against any of the property of a creditor; (e) the issuance of a subpoena or order, in supplementary proceedings, against or with respect to any of the property of a creditor; or (f) the issuance of a warrant of attachment against any of the property of a creditor, to set off and apply against any indebtedness, whether matured or unmatured, of such creditor to such debtor, any amount owing from such debtor to such creditor, at or at any time after, the happening of any of the above mentioned events, and the aforesaid right of set off may be exercised by such debtor against such creditor or against any trustee in bankruptcy, debtor in possession, assignee for the benefit of creditors, receiver or execution, judgment or attachment creditor of such creditor, or against anyone else claiming through or against such creditor or such trustee in bankruptcy, debtor in possession, assignee for the benefit of creditors, receivers, or execution, judgment or attachment creditor, notwithstanding the fact that such right of set off shall not have been exercised by such debtor prior to the making, filing or issuance, or service upon such debtor of, or of notice of, any such petition; assignment for the benefit of creditors; appointment or application for the appointment of a receiver; or issuance of execution,
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'irrevocably' weakens the waiver of set-off rights, creating a potential ambiguity. NY DCD § 151 provides debtors with the right to set off against unmatured debts under certain conditions like bankruptcy or insolvency. By removing 'irrevocably,' the contract now lacks the clear intent to waive these setoff rights, particularly if financial distress arises. This makes the enforceability of the waiver questionable, as one party could argue that the waiver was not intended to be permanent. The inclusion of 'irrevocably' would show the intent for the contract to hold, even if insolvency issues arise, supporting a clear contradiction in enforceability.
